title: Where AI Struggles
intro: You're building a small e-commerce app and you've been using AI for everything. Things have been going great -- until they aren't. Imagine a beautiful bridge with a hidden crack in a support beam -- AI code can look perfect on the surface but hide dangerous flaws. A customer reports they were charged twice for the same order.

teach.title: AI Code Can Be Like a Beautiful Bridge With Weak Foundations
teach.body: AI-generated code often looks clean and professional on the surface. But underneath, it might have subtle problems -- like a bridge that looks great but has a crack in a support beam. The code compiles, the tests pass, but there's a logic error waiting to cause real damage.

teach.title: AI Is Like a Spell Checker That Can't Read
teach.body: A spell checker catches typos perfectly but has no idea if your sentence actually makes sense. It can fix 'teh' to 'the' but won't notice if you said 'the car flew to the grocery store.' AI-generated code is similar -- it looks syntactically correct and follows conventions, but may contain logic that makes no real-world sense.

teach.title: Common AI Failure Modes
teach.body:
AI-generated code commonly fails in these ways:

1. **Duplicate logic** -- AI loses track during editing and repeats operations
2. **Missing edge cases** -- it handles the happy path but not errors or empty inputs
3. **Outdated patterns** -- it uses APIs or syntax from old library versions
4. **Confident but wrong** -- the code looks correct but has subtle logic bugs

teach.title: The Critical Code Rule
teach.body: Any code that handles **money, security, or user data** needs extra scrutiny. AI is great for scaffolding and UI, but payment processing, authentication, and data handling should always be reviewed line by line by a human. Add tests as safety nets for critical paths.
Knowing AI's weak spots doesn't slow you down -- it speeds you up. You'll catch bugs before your users do, which means fewer refunds, better reviews, and more trust in the products you ship.
AI code can look perfect and still have subtle bugs. Always review critical logic yourself, especially anything involving money, security, or user data. Write tests for important code paths.

start.narrative: A customer emails that they were charged $49.99 twice for a single order. You check the database and confirm the duplicate charge. The checkout code was written entirely by AI. What's your first step?

choices.text: Read through the AI-generated checkout code yourself
Ask AI to fix the double-charge bug
Refund the customer and hope it doesn't happen again
Remove the duplicate chargeCard call and add an idempotency key as a safeguard
Ask AI to rewrite the whole checkout flow

ask-ai-fix.narrative: You paste the code into AI and ask it to fix the double charge. The AI says 'I don't see any issues with this code, it looks correct.' AI can't always spot its own mistakes, especially when the code is syntactically valid. The duplicate call is logically wrong but structurally fine.

choices.text: Read through the code yourself line by line

just-refund.narrative: You refund the customer, but the next day three more customers report the same issue. The bug is still there. Ignoring the root cause just delays the inevitable -- and costs your business money and trust.

choices.text: Okay, time to actually look at the code

add-safeguard.narrative: You've removed the duplicate chargeCard call. The immediate bug is gone, but if a future AI edit (or a tired human) re-introduces the same pattern, the same customers get billed twice again. What defense do you add at the layer below the function?

choices.text: Add an idempotency key on the order so chargeCard refuses to run twice for the same orderId, even if called twice
Add a runtime guard inside chargeCard that throws if the order has already been charged in the last 60 seconds
Add a code comment '// do not call twice' above the call site
Skip the safeguard -- you trust yourself not to reintroduce the bug

add-tests.narrative: Safeguard is in place. Now you need a regression test that fails loudly the next time something in the checkout flow tries to charge twice. What test do you write?

choices.text: An integration test that runs the full checkout flow against a fake payment gateway and asserts chargeCard was called exactly once per order
A unit test that mocks chargeCard and asserts the spy was called exactly once when checkout() runs once
A snapshot test of the checkout function source -- if anyone changes it, the snapshot fails
Skip the test -- the safeguard is enough

review-policy.narrative: Bug fixed, safeguard installed, regression test in place. The deeper question: how do you keep AI from quietly shipping the next version of this bug in another critical path? You want a policy you'll actually follow, not a wish.

choices.text: Tag files that touch money/auth/PII with a CODEOWNERS rule so PRs touching them require explicit human review, plus mandatory tests for critical paths
Promise yourself you'll review AI output more carefully from now on
Stop using AI for any backend code

end-best.narrative: Money-handling code now has CODEOWNERS-enforced human review and required test coverage on every PR. AI still writes the boilerplate, scaffolding, and UI -- but the next double-charge bug can't slip through unnoticed. The original incident becomes the last of its kind, not the first of many.
end-best.feedback: Right call from end to end. You read the code yourself, removed the bug, hardened the layer below it, added a regression test that catches the failure pattern, and made the review policy automatic instead of aspirational. AI keeps its speed-up where it's safe; humans keep the gate where mistakes cost money.

end-okay.narrative: Banning AI from backend work prevents this specific class of bug but throws away a lot of legitimate productivity. The real failure was the lack of review and tests on critical paths, not the use of AI itself.
end-okay.feedback: Overcorrection. The lesson isn't 'no AI in backend' -- it's 'critical paths get human review and tests, regardless of who wrote the first draft.' AI can still speed up validation, queries, and scaffolding in the backend; the gate just goes around money/auth/data, not around the tool.

end-vague.narrative: Two months later, AI inserts a subtle off-by-one in the loyalty-points calculation. You meant to review carefully but were on a deadline. The same class of bug ships again because nothing structural changed.
end-vague.feedback: 'Be more careful' is the same advice you got after the first incident. Personal vigilance doesn't survive sprint pressure. Make the review structural -- CODEOWNERS, required tests on critical paths, mandatory reviewers -- so the policy holds even on your worst day.
